---
title: "Bob Week"
output: 
  html_document: 
    theme: cosmo
    css: style.css
---

I am a PhD candidate at the University of Idaho working with professor [Scott Nuismer](http://www.webpages.uidaho.edu/~snuismer/Nuismer_Lab/). My degree program is [Bioinformatics and Computational Biology](http://www.uidaho.edu/sci/bcb) (BCB) which is housed within [The Institute for Bioinformatics and Evolutionary Studies](http://www.ibest.uidaho.edu/) (IBEST).

My interests are in the patterns of traits and the interspecific interactions they mediate within ecological communities. In particular, I study how these patterns have and are evolving and their implications for the ecosystems in which they are imbedded. My long term goal as a scientist is to contribute to a unified understanding of evolutionary ecosystem ecology that transcends the scales of time, space, and biological diversity. My general approach is bottom-up, starting with mathematical models of local scale processes as the basic building blocks and deriving from them properties of larger scale systems. As a mathematician my goal is to extend and generalize a unified formal theory of evolution and ecology, capturing diverse sets of processes within an internally consistent mathematical language. By doing so it is my hope that the theory I contribute to will become as synthetic and interwoven as the understanding of nature I am pursuing.

My proposed thesis focuses on contributing to a coevolutionary theory of community ecology in the context of plant-pollinator networks. Specifically, I am working on methods to detect various forms of coevolution in nature, models that predict the coevolution of patterns of interspecific interactions and the evolution of the phenology of ecological networks. The theory of stochastic differential equations is my primary weapon for confronting these tasks. However, working with empiricists such as [Paul CaraDonna](http://www.pauljcaradonna.com/) keeps me tethered to reality through fruitful conversations and excellent data.

My CV is available [here](cv.pdf).
